ShimoMura是日本最大的Yakuza犯罪家族山口古山的成员。当他的一个上级询问他，如果他想堆积一堆速度，他自然地说是的。这是2016年5月14日，Shimomura住在名古屋市。三十二岁和瘦的瘦弱，具有富有尖锐的眼睛，他的外表骄傲，往往穿着西装和镜面吹过的鞋带。但他是组织中的一个未成年人物：债务收藏家，是奇数就业的表演者。
 卓越的向他保证，该计划的风险很低，并指示他参加了那个晚上在名古屋的酒吧会议。 （ShimoMura离开山口古墓以来，要求只有他的姓氏被称为他的姓氏。）当Shimomura出现时，他发现了三个其他歹徒，他没有人知道。像许多Yakuza一样，他是韩国血统，其他两个也是韩国日本;有一段时间，他们在韩国讲话。优越的终于到了，五个人搬进了一个私人房间。每个志愿者都有一个普通的白色信用卡。卡上没有芯片，没有数字，没有名字 - 只是一个磁带。
  来自薄手册的高级读取说明：第二天早上，周日，他们应该去任何711岁，并在商店的A..m中使用他们的白卡。他们无法使用普通银行A.T.M.或一个在另一家便利店中。歹徒应该每次撤回十万日元（大约九百美元），但每台机器不超过19亿。如果有人从单一的一个a.t.m提取，他的卡将被阻止。提款可以从5开始。 。并继续直到8。 。志愿者被告知当促进时选择日语语言 - 表明，Shimomura意识到，卡片是外国的。在进行十九次提款后，他们应该在访问另外711英寸之前等一小时。他们可以保留10％的现金。其余的会去老板。最后，每个志愿者都被告知要记住一个。
  周日早晨，夏梅村早期上涨，穿着牛仔裤，太阳镜，棒球帽和一件旧T恤。他走到7-11岁，在那里他买了一场米饭球和焦炭，以解决自己。他将卡片插入了A.M.当屏幕问他喜欢哪种语言时，他在选择“日语”时感到震惊神经。他退出了十万日元，然后是另一个，然后是另一个。除了登记册的家伙外，商店里没有其他人在店里，谁对他似乎没有兴趣。
 在第一次退出后，ShimoMura打印了收据。他在纸上看到了外国名字 - 他无法讲述这个名字的国籍，但他知道这不是日本人 - 然后在口袋里塞了收据。左右8岁，在该地区的几个A..M.完成了38个提款，他回家了，因为他的凸起口袋而蹒跚地咆哮：380万日元是很多现金。 Shimomura花了他的十分大约三十五百美元 - 并在公寓的抽屉里藏起了它。 3，他遇到了他的优越，提供剩下的钱。 （后来，他发现另一个歹徒已经潜逃了钱和卡。）
 高级告诉秋千米，他会保留他志愿者所带来的5％，并将其余的现金送到他的老板。当Shimomura移交他的钱时，他感觉到了上级旨在赢得了许多人。他是对的。随着报纸很快报道，早晨，在日本横跨日本的大约170亿美元，超过六百万美元撤回了超过六百万美元。报纸推出的是，7-11岁的目标是因为他们是日本唯一的便利店，其现金终端都接受了外国卡。突袭后不久，该国在该国的撤出限额将减少到五万日元。
  Shimomura推断他一直在骗局中的食物链底部。真正的金钱制造商更高了。他不知道的是，直到去年接受采访这本杂志，是链条顶部的恶棍的身份。在A.T.M之后不久据日本警察称，盗窃7  - 十一行动的林杰尔从中国越过朝鲜。 Shimomura毫不道偿地为朝鲜人民军队收集资金，作为被称为快餐的球拍的一部分。

到朝鲜的第三次重大袭击的时间，没有人发现该政权的网络威胁再次搞笑。在美国，欧洲和亚洲的Wannacry 2.0被称为Wannacry 2.0的赎金软件方案 - 包括波音，英国国家卫生服务和德国联邦铁路的计算机系统。黑客加密电脑后电脑，然后在比特币上要求支付，解冻系统。朝鲜人量身定制了一些赎金软件代码，然后通过批准一个被称为ETERERELBLUE的危险的美国代码来从一个设备传播到下一个设备，这是一个刑事组织致电暗影经纪人从N.S.A偷走了。然后在线发布。
 来自父母家的卧室的英格兰的二十两岁的黑客和恶意软件专家，分析了Wannacry代码，并弄清楚如何指导它产生的大部分交通“陷阱” - 恶意软件不会伤害的Web地址。在Hutchins意识到他已经上长了黑客后，有线报道，他上楼告诉他的家人。他的母亲是一名护士，正在砍洋葱。 “干得好，甜心，”她说，在回到她的烹饪之前。
 朝鲜政权长期以来一直被认为是一个根本犯罪的企业。 Joseph Bermudez，Jr.是战略和国际研究中心的高级研究员，告诉我，该国的生存始终由黑手党的“惠顾制度”是基础。他解释说，即使在朝鲜战争面前，走私者和军阀曾在该地区繁殖。自从D.P.R.K的诞生以来，犯罪已被用来加纳，而不仅是制度的现金，而且还用于政治和社会资本。贝尔梅德兹所说，金斯说，培养了“渴望为领导者产生充收的收入。”
 直到近期，朝鲜最有利可图的国家赞助的刑事运营包括借水卷烟，创造伪造金，濒危物种的交易，以及制造和分配实验室制造的非法药物如甲基苯丙胺。在七十年代，朝鲜外交官经常被贩运的麻醉品。八十年代，朝鲜造假者创造了一个非常合理的百元“超级”。 （2006年，秘密服务估计，它从流通中删除了五十百万美元的假票据;七年后，美国国债重新设计了百美元的钞票，额外的安全功能。）许多传统的刑事收入流继续流动回到平壤，但在过去十年中，国家的焦点已经枢转到了互联网。
  朝鲜数字犯罪狂欢的范围和创造力捕获了许多守卫。平壤的网络战士不仅可以危及世界各地的计算机网络;他们在利用新技术方面表现出真正的创新。 Luke Dembosky是一名咨询互联网安全问题的律师，在索尼黑客时，曾在索尼·赫克斯的北朝鲜的网络威胁上，当时他是司法部的国家安全部门的副助理律师。然后他目睹了孟加拉国的赫斯特 - 一种醒目的飞跃。 “尽管这项业务很多，但是，在这项业务中，这对像我这样的人令人惊叹的是，看到一个相对孤立的国家演员而不是简单地抄袭别人的方法或计划，而是实际打破新的地面，”他说。
  哈佛分析师Priscilla Moriuchi告诉我，回想起来，D.P.R.K.转向网络犯罪一直是有机发展。 “朝鲜人识别犯罪，”她说。 “他们融入了许多地方，许多地方有这个犯罪和灰色的地下世界。因此，覆盖这项新技术，互联网是自然的。它将犯罪组织和走私者彼此联系起来。“
 我们讨论了日本人A.T.M. 2016年的诈骗。Shimomura可能没有知道他的终极老板，但Yakuza几十年来一直在朝鲜出来违法。朝鲜围绕千年，朝鲜提供约40％的日本的甲基苯丙胺。因此，如果平壤中的网络诈骗者需要在地面上靴子在名古屋提取现金，他们可以提出要求，并很快就会得到回答。 
Moriuchi还指出，虽然朝鲜黑客在技术上完成了，但他们更重要的属性是一种令人兴奋的狂欢 - 童话。 在孟加拉国银行案件中，劫匪在达卡在达卡举行之前等了十七个月，他们在他们撤离了他的哈巴。 他们确定了理想的周末和假期罢工; 他们计划如何快速将现金赶出收件人银行; 他们选择了特别是LAX认识您的客户协议的机构。 一旦他们执行了盗窃，他们将在菲律宾使用本地承包商来洗涤他们的比索，减轻  ......

Shimomura was a member of the Yamaguchi-gumi, the largest  yakuza crime family in Japan. When one of his superiors asked him if he wanted to make a pile of fast money, he naturally said yes. It was May 14, 2016, and Shimomura was living in the city of Nagoya. Thirty-two years old and skinny, with expressive eyes, he took pride in his appearance, often wearing a suit and mirror-shined loafers. But he was a minor figure in the organization: a collector of debts, a performer of odd jobs.
 The superior assured him that the scheme was low risk, and instructed him to attend a meeting that evening at a bar in Nagoya. (Shimomura, who has since left the Yamaguchi-gumi, asked to be referred to only by his surname.) When Shimomura showed up, he found three other gangsters, none of whom he knew. Like many yakuza, he is of Korean descent, and two of the others were also Korean-Japanese; for a while, they spoke in Korean. The superior finally arrived, and the five men moved into a private room. Each volunteer was given a plain white credit card. There was no chip on the card, no numbers, no name—just a magnetic strip.
  The superior read instructions from a thin manual: early the next morning, a Sunday, they should go to any 7-Eleven and use their white card at the store’s A.T.M. They could not use a regular bank A.T.M., or one in another convenience store. The gangsters should each withdraw a hundred thousand yen at a time (about nine hundred dollars) but make no more than nineteen transactions per machine. If anybody made twenty withdrawals from a single A.T.M., his card would be blocked. Withdrawals could start at 5  . . and continue until 8  . . The volunteers were told to choose the Japanese language when prompted—an indication, Shimomura realized, that the cards were foreign. After making nineteen withdrawals, they should wait an hour before visiting another 7-Eleven. They could keep ten per cent of the cash. The rest would go to the bosses. Finally, each volunteer was told to memorize a  .
  On Sunday morning, Shimomura rose early, and dressed in jeans, sunglasses, a baseball cap, and an old T-shirt. He walked to a 7-Eleven, where he bought a rice ball and a Coke, to settle himself. He inserted the card into the A.T.M. When the screen asked him which language he preferred, he felt a tremor of nerves while selecting “Japanese.” He withdrew a hundred thousand yen, then another, and then another. There was nobody else in the store apart from the guy at the register, who didn’t seem interested in him.
 After making the first withdrawal, Shimomura printed a receipt. He saw a foreign name on the paper—he couldn’t tell what nationality the name was, but he knew it wasn’t Japanese—then stuffed the receipt in his pocket. Around 8  , having completed a total of thirty-eight withdrawals at several A.T.M.s in the area, he headed home, waddling because of his bulging pockets: 3.8 million yen is a lot of cash. Shimomura took his ten per cent—about thirty-five hundred dollars—and stashed it in a drawer in his apartment. At 3  , he met his superior to deliver the remaining money. (Later, he discovered that one of the other gangsters had absconded with the money and the card.)
 The superior told Shimomura that he would retain five per cent of what his volunteers brought in and send the rest of the cash to his bosses. When Shimomura handed over his money, he sensed that the superior had enlisted many others. He was right. As the newspapers soon reported, more than sixteen million dollars was withdrawn from roughly seventeen hundred 7-Eleven A.T.M.s across Japan that morning, using data stolen from South Africa’s Standard Bank. The newspapers surmised that 7-Elevens had been targeted because they were the only convenience stores in Japan whose cash terminals all accepted foreign cards. Soon after the raids, the withdrawal limit for many A.T.M.s in the country was reduced to fifty thousand yen.
  Shimomura deduced that he had been at the bottom of the food chain in the scam. The real money-makers were much higher up. What he did not know, until an interview with this magazine last year, was the identity of the villains at the top of the chain. Shortly after the A.T.M. thefts, according to Japanese police, the ringleader of the 7-Eleven operation crossed from China into North Korea. Shimomura had unwittingly been collecting money for the Korean People’s Army, as part of a racket that became known as FASTCash.
 In satellite images of East Asia at night, lights blare almost everywhere, except in one inky patch between the Yellow Sea and the Sea of Japan, and between the thirty-eighth and the forty-third parallels: North Korea. Only Pyongyang, the capital, emits a recognizably modern glow. The dark country is one of the last nominally Communist nations in the world—a Stalinist personality cult centered on  Kim Jong Un, the peevish, ruthless scion of the dynasty that has ruled North Korea since 1948, after the peninsula was divided. The D.P.R.K. purports to be a socialist autarky founded on the principle of  juche, or self-reliance. Its borders are closed and its people sequestered. Foreigners find it profoundly difficult to understand what is happening inside North Korea, but it is even harder for ordinary North Korean citizens to learn about the outside world. A tiny fraction of one per cent of North Koreans has access to the Internet.
 Yet, paradoxically, the North Korean government has produced some of the world’s most proficient hackers. At first glance, the situation is perverse, even comical—like Jamaica winning an Olympic gold in bobsledding—but the cyber threat from North Korea is real and growing. Like many countries, including the United States, North Korea has equipped its military with offensive and intelligence-gathering cyber weapons. In 2016, for instance, military coders from Pyongyang stole more than two hundred gigabytes of South Korean Army data, which included documents known as Operational Plan 5015—a detailed analysis of how a war with the country’s northern neighbor might proceed, and, notably, a plot to “decapitate” North Korea by assassinating Kim Jong Un. The breach was so egregious that Kim Tae-woo, a former president of the Korea Institute for National Unification, a think tank in Seoul,  told the  Financial Times, “Part of my mind hopes the South Korean military intentionally leaked the classified documents to the North with the intention of having a second strategy.”
  North Korea, moreover, is the only nation in the world whose government is known to conduct nakedly criminal hacking for monetary gain. Units of its military-intelligence division, the Reconnaissance General Bureau, are trained specifically for this purpose. In 2013, Kim Jong Un described the men who worked in the “brave R.G.B.” as his “warriors . . . for the construction of a strong and prosperous nation.”
  North Korea’s cybercrime program is hydra-headed, with tactics ranging from bank heists to the deployment of ransomware and the theft of cryptocurrency from online exchanges. It is difficult to quantify how successful Pyongyang’s hackers have been. Unlike terrorist groups, North Korea’s cybercriminals do not claim responsibility when they strike, and the government issues reflexive denials. As a result, even seasoned observers sometimes disagree when attributing individual attacks to North Korea. Nevertheless, in 2019, a United Nations panel of experts on sanctions against North Korea  issued a report estimating that the country had raised two billion dollars through cybercrime. Since the report was written, there has been bountiful evidence to indicate that the pace and the ingenuity of North Korea’s online threat have accelerated.
 According to the U.N., many of the funds stolen by North Korean hackers are spent on the Korean People’s Army’s weapons program, including its development of nuclear missiles. The cybercrime spree has also been a cheap and effective way of circumventing the harsh sanctions that have long been imposed on the country. In February, John C. Demers, the Assistant Attorney General for the National Security Division of the Justice Department, declared that North Korea, “using keyboards rather than guns,” had become a “criminal syndicate with a flag.”
 North Korea’s leaders have been attuned to the nefarious opportunities of a connected world since at least the early nineteen-nineties. A  2019 paper on the regime, written by scholars at Korea University, in Seoul, notes that  Kim Jong Il, having watched the United States’ military engagement in the two Gulf conflicts, concluded that “modern war is decided by one’s conduct of electronic warfare.” (Among other tactics, American planes jammed Iraqi radar systems.) In 2005, a Korean People’s Army book quoted Kim as saying, “If the Internet is like a gun, cyberattacks are like atomic bombs.” His son Kim Jong Un came to power in 2012 and saw the commercial potential of the technology, noting that his army could “penetrate any sanctions.” Cyber prowess, he soon declared, was an “all-purpose sword that guarantees the North Korean People’s Armed Forces ruthless striking capability, along with nuclear weapons and missiles.” Yet the West didn’t really wake up to the danger posed by North Korea’s cyber forces until after the country executed three spectacular crimes, between 2014 and 2017.
 The first was a hack of Sony Pictures. In June, 2014, Sony released a trailer for “ The Interview,” a Seth Rogen and James Franco comedy about hapless journalists recruited by the C.I.A. to assassinate Kim Jong Un. A spokesperson for the regime called the film a “wanton act of terror” and promised a “merciless response” if the studio proceeded with releasing the film. Sony pressed ahead. (Rogen joked on Twitter, “People don’t usually wanna kill me for one of my movies until after they’ve paid 12 bucks for it.”)
 That November, Sony employees reported that their computers had been hacked, by a group calling itself Guardians of Peace. After many of the company’s computers froze, Sony shut down the rest, stanching the bleed of data that was under way. For a few days, Sony Pictures operated without an electronic network, and in subsequent weeks the hackers leaked embarrassing—and, in some cases, damaging—e-mails, salaries, medical records, movies, and screenplays belonging to the company and its employees. Five upcoming Sony films were put online, as was the script of the next James Bond movie, “ Spectre.” One of the studio heads, Amy Pascal, resigned after the hackers posted e-mails in which she joked with the producer Scott Rudin that at a meeting with President  Barack Obama she’d be smart to bring up movies about slavery.
 The F.B.I. soon attributed the attack to North Korean state actors. Pyongyang denied involvement but declared the hack a “righteous deed.” Obama promised to “respond proportionally” to what he called an act of “cyber vandalism.” Michael McCaul, who chaired the House Homeland Security Committee, later told reporters that the U.S. had launched a number of “cyber responses” to the Sony hack, not least a ten-hour Internet outage in North Korea in December, 2014.
  If the attack on Sony had a cartoonish quality, the second major North Korean attack was like a caper. Around the time that the hackers were breaking into Sony’s network, members of the same gang—which became known as the Lazarus Group—began scoping out banks in Dhaka, Bangladesh. Accounts linked to the Lazarus Group sent e-mails to an array of targets at Bangladesh Bank and other financial institutions in Dhaka. The messages contained a link to malware that, if clicked, granted the North Koreans access to internal computer systems. In the first two months of 2015, at least three Bangladesh Bank employees were lured by these “spear-phishing” e-mails into downloading the infected attachment. By that March, the hackers had established a “backdoor” within the bank’s electronic communication system, allowing them to send messages to one another in a way that mimicked the bank’s encrypted-communication protocols, and did not alert security to their presence. The hidden hackers then spent ten months learning about Bangladesh Bank’s operations from the inside.
 Like many national banks in developing countries, Bangladesh Bank holds a foreign-currency account with the Federal Reserve bank in New York. On February 4, 2016, the Federal Reserve received instructions from Bangladesh Bank to make dozens of payments, totalling nearly a billion dollars, to various accounts, including one in Sri Lanka and four in the Philippines. The requests were made via the   network—a global conduit for money transfers, based near Brussels. In fact, the Lazarus hackers had sent the requests, using stolen usernames and passwords that they had collected while roaming around Bangladesh Bank’s network. In their fraudulent messages to the Federal Reserve, the Lazarus members had incorporated many details from genuine, previously executed   transfers, so that it would not be obvious their own requests were bogus. To further cover their tracks, the hackers had installed a network update that blocked   messages from being read at Bangladesh Bank—a piece of legerdemain that later impressed security experts. It was the equivalent of breaking into a bank’s vault after disabling its surveillance cameras.
 Priscilla Moriuchi, a fellow at Harvard’s Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs who focusses on the North Korean cyber threat, worked at the National Security Agency for twelve years. She told me that the Bangladesh operation was “flashy.” But the robbers not only showed technical finesse, she said; their patient work in the Dhaka heist “signalled a larger tactical and operational maturity.”
 The Federal Reserve granted the first five payment requests, a total of a hundred and one million dollars. The next thirty payments, which amounted to eight hundred and fifty million dollars, stalled only because of a stroke of luck. An automated alert system was activated after detecting, in the text of a transfer request, the word “Jupiter,” which happened to be in the address of a Philippines bank branch. This alert was tripped because an unrelated business, Jupiter Seaways Shipping, in Athens, was on a sanctions-evasion watch list for its activities relating to Iran.
  After this and another small irregularity were detected, freeze requests were placed on the recipient accounts. But—as the hackers had anticipated—because the heist was carried out on a holiday weekend in the Philippines the freeze requests weren’t processed for another forty-eight hours. By that time, some eighty-one million dollars had been transferred into a different account. Most of this money was then withdrawn, converted into cash as Philippine pesos, and exchanged for casino chips. At the time, gambling establishments in the Philippines were exempt from anti-money-laundering regulations. It wasn’t a billion dollars, but it was a huge haul.
 By the time of North Korea’s third major attack, nobody found the regime’s cyber threat funny anymore. A 2017 ransomware scheme known as Wannacry 2.0 crippled networks in America, Europe, and Asia—including the computer systems of Boeing, Britain’s National Health Service, and Germany’s federal railway. The hackers encrypted computer after computer, then demanded payment, in bitcoin, to unfreeze the systems. North Koreans tailored some ransomware code and then propagated it from one device to the next by appropriating a dangerous piece of American code, known as EternalBlue, that a criminal group calling itself the Shadow Brokers had stolen from the N.S.A. and then posted online.
 A twenty-two-year-old hacker and malware expert from England named Marcus Hutchins, who worked out of a bedroom in his parents’ house, analyzed the Wannacry code and figured out how to direct much of the traffic that it was generating into a “sinkhole”—a Web address where the malware would do no harm. After Hutchins realized that he had upended the hack,  Wired  reported, he went upstairs to tell his family. His mother, a nurse, was chopping onions. “Well done, sweetheart,” she said, before returning to her cooking.
 The North Korean regime has long been considered a fundamentally criminal enterprise. Joseph Bermudez, Jr., a senior fellow at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, told me that the country’s survival has always been underpinned by a mafia-like “patronage system.” He explained that, even before the Korean War, smugglers and warlords had thrived in the region. Since the birth of the D.P.R.K., crime has been used to garner not only cash for the regime but also political and social capital. The Kims, Bermudez said, have fostered a “desire to produce revenue to secure pleasure with the leader.”
 Until recently, North Korea’s most lucrative state-sponsored criminal operations included the smuggling of cigarettes, the creation of counterfeit money, the trading of endangered species, and the manufacture and distribution of laboratory-made illegal drugs such as methamphetamine. In the seventies, North Korean diplomats who were posted abroad often trafficked narcotics. In the eighties, North Korean counterfeiters created a remarkably plausible hundred-dollar “supernote.” (In 2006, the Secret Service  estimated that it had removed fifty million dollars’ worth of fake notes from circulation; seven years later, the U.S. Treasury redesigned its hundred-dollar bill with extra security features.) Many traditional criminal revenue streams continue to flow back to Pyongyang, but in the past decade the state’s focus has pivoted to the Internet.
  The range and creativity of North Korea’s digital crime spree caught many off guard. It wasn’t just that Pyongyang’s cyber warriors could compromise computer networks around the world; they showed real innovation in exploiting new technologies. Luke Dembosky, an attorney who advises companies on Internet-security issues, first confronted North Korea’s cyber threat at the time of the Sony hack, when he was the Deputy Assistant Attorney General in the Justice Department’s National Security Division. Then he witnessed the Bangladesh heist—a striking leap in sophistication. “It was stunning for someone like me, despite years in this business, to see a relatively isolated nation-state actor not simply copying someone else’s methodology or scheme but actually breaking new ground,” he said.
  Priscilla Moriuchi, the Harvard analyst, told me that, in retrospect, the D.P.R.K.’s turn to cybercrime had been an organic development. “North Koreans understand criminality,” she said. “They’re integrated in many, many places with this criminal and gray underground world. And so it’s natural to overlay this new technology, the Internet. It connects criminal organizations and smugglers with one another.”
 We discussed the Japanese A.T.M. scam of 2016. Shimomura may not have known his ultimate boss, but the yakuza had been smuggling illegal products out of North Korea for decades. Around the turn of the millennium, North Korea supplied about forty per cent of Japan’s methamphetamines. So, if cyber scammers in Pyongyang needed boots on the ground to withdraw cash in Nagoya, they could make a request, and it would soon be answered.
 Moriuchi also noted that, although the North Korean hackers were technically accomplished, their more important attribute was a felonious savoir-faire. In the Bangladesh Bank case, the robbers waited seventeen months after their first reconnaissance in Dhaka before they pulled off the heist. They had determined the ideal weekend and holiday to strike; they had planned how to move cash quickly out of recipient banks; and they had chosen institutions that had particularly lax know-your-customer protocols. Once they executed the theft, they used local contractors in the Philippines to launder their pesos, effectivel
......

Amazon reinvented supermarket shopping with its  checkout-less Go stores, now the e-commerce giant is applying its tech prowess to hairdressing. The company has chosen east London as the home for its  first-ever salon, a 1,500 square foot concept site where customers can try on virtual hair colors using an augmented reality color bar and shop curated  beauty products using displays and QR codes.
 The AR tech relies on a tablet embedded in a mirror to show patrons how they'd look in a range of different shades using the slate's front-facing camera. In that sense, it looks similar to the  Live Mode make-up feature Amazon introduced on its mobile app in 2019, which allowed users to experiment with different lipstick colors. Meanwhile, the shopping experience utilizes Amazon's new "point-and-learn" technology, allowing customers to point to products on a shelf to view relevant info — including brand videos — on an accompanying screen. You'll then be able to buy the items by scanning a QR code to visit the product page on Amazon's UK site.
   Tech aside, the salon will offer traditional styling from the professionals at Neville Hair and Beauty, a London-based boutique that normally charges between £50 to £250 ($70 to $350) for adult cuts and treatments. The retailer is currently trying out the concept on its employees and plans to open it to the public in the coming weeks.
 Amazon's salon arrives on the heels of its  first UK convenience stores, which offer the same checkout-free tech as its Go stores, with three Amazon Fresh sites currently operating across London. For now, the company says it has no plans to launch additional salons. Instead, it views the concept as a testing ground for new tech and a showcase for its beauty products.

note_en:
Munin is a networked resource monitoring tool that can help analyze resource trends and "what just happened				to kill our performance?" problems. It is designed to be very plug and play. A default installation provides				a lot of graphs with almost no work.
  In Norse mythology Hugin and Munin are the ravens of the god king Odin. They flew all over Midgard for him,				seeing and remembering, and later telling him. "Munin" means "memory".
    Check the demo server
  Munin polls your servers once every 5 minutes. Don't wait any longer to be notified when something							goes wrong
    Munin will cleverly choose from 500+ available plugins to monitor every service running on							your server
  Write your own plugins							in the language of your choice to supervise whatever you want
  Munin the monitoring tool surveys all your computers and remembers what it saw. It presents all the				information in graphs through a web interface. Its emphasis is on plug and play capabilities. After				completing an installation, a high number of monitoring plugins will be playing with no more effort.
  Using Munin you can easily monitor the performance of your computers, networks, SANs, applications,				weather measurements and whatever comes to mind. It makes it easy to determine "what's different today"				when a performance problem crops up. It makes it easy to see how you're doing capacity-wise on any resources.
  Munin uses the excellent  ​RRDTool (written				by Tobi Oetiker) and the framework is written in Perl, while plugins may be written in any language.				Munin has a master/node architecture in which the master connects to all the nodes at regular intervals				and asks them for data. It then stores the data in RRD files, and (if needed) updates the graphs. One of				the main goals has been ease of creating new plugins (graphs).
    Plugins are small, standalone executables. They are easy to write, and can be written in any language.				You can find many user contributed plugins in ​the  contrib repository				or  easily write your own ones.

中国政府在武汉实验室造成了全世界完全透明。我们需要知道如何尽可能安全地进行病毒研究。
     自1919年西班牙流感以来，我们现在已经进入了最严重的全球大流行病，我们仍然不知道病毒的起源。如果我们想防止下一个流行病，那就找出了。但是，这些努力在政治和不完整的信息中占据。
 在大流行的关键周，北京压制了基本信息并阻止了世界卫生组织调查人员进入该国。当中国生物学家勇敢地发布病毒在线测序的基因组时，他的实验室被关闭了“整改”。
  然而，我们不知道武汉的尸冠病毒，导致疾病来自，或者如何对人类致命和传播。自2013年以来，云南省蝙蝠收集了病毒最接近的亲属。科维德 -  19次于2019年12月在武汉市报道，距离云南超过1000英里。美国国家过敏和传染病研究所主任安东尼Fauci博士表示，该来源最有可能在武汉成为动物“潮湿市场”;疾病控制和预防中心的前总监Robert Redfield博士认为，武汉中武汉的中国病毒学院最有可能来自武汉，正国议员迈克庞培。
  在初步报告中，世界卫生组织报告说，实验室泄漏是“极其不可能的”。然而，世卫组织总干事Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus警告说，该团队在潜在的实验室泄漏中的探针受到中国政府的限制，需要进一步调查。总干事希望派专家回到中国病毒学研究所或WIV，更完全探索实验室泄漏的可能性。
   自2018年流行病前的国家部门电缆是相关的，提出了该设施的外交专家涉及不被录得适当的安全措施。其中一条电​​缆警告说，WIV是一个Biosafety-4实验室，即最级别的生物保护认证，正在研究基于BAT的冠状病毒，并且由于研究人员表明，有新的SARS的风险是如此各种SARS样冠状病毒可以与为SARS-CORONA病毒鉴定的人受体（ACE2）相互作用。国家部门随后宣布，WIV实验室的研究人员报告了2019年秋季的流感样症状，中国当局承认爆发前几个月。
  武汉实验室正在进行蝙蝠冠状病毒研究是无可争议的。我们知道这一点是因为在奥巴马政府期间，Fauuci授权美国对蝙蝠病毒进行WIV研究的资助2015年的一个近70万美元的国家卫生研究院。具体而言，Fauuci允许赋予eCohealth转基因基于蝙蝠的冠状动脉绕过HHS Pandemic病原体控制和监督，或P3Co，审查委员会，其目的是监测可能使病毒更具传染性的病毒研究，所谓的职能研究。倡导者说，这些工作可以帮助了解危险新兴病原体的理解和预测，以及其他好处，但明显的危险使得研究争议。 
2014年在2011年全球科学露头的脚跟上暂停了2014年暂停了职能研究，当威斯康星州和荷兰有意地将H5N1禽流感病毒有效地修改H5N1禽流感病毒跳跃物种。
 Fauci外包病毒研究对中国的武汉实验室并获得了实验室，继续接受美国联邦资金。国家过敏和传染病研究所在2017年形成的P3Co委员会出于潜在的危险研究，而且Fauuci通过豁免审查研究资助的eCohealth豁免酌情审查。尼亚德说这不是职能研究，而其他科学家则比赛，并表示应该被P3CO审查。
   2015年在自然医学杂志中分享了研究人员的结论，他调查了称为SHC014的病毒，这些病毒在中国蝙蝠中发现。它们创造了由SHC014的表面蛋白组成的病毒，其中SARS病毒的骨干适应在小鼠中生长并模仿人类疾病。这种嵌合体感染了人气通风电池，证明SHC014的表面蛋白质具有必要的结构，以在人体细胞上结合受体并感染它们。
  Ecohealth有一种操纵基于蝙蝠病毒的历史。其总统彼得·达斯扎克在2019年12月的第一个报告的Covid-19案例之前短暂地从新加坡举行的播客中的播客中：“你可以轻松地操纵他们的实验室。尖峰蛋白驱动了很多因电晕病毒的风险发生了很多会发生的事情。所以你可以得到序列，你可以构建蛋白质...插入另一个病毒的骨干，在实验室中做一些工作。“
  Rutgers大学化学生物学教授Richard H. eSBright认为，在确定Ecohealth Grant不涉及增强中国蝙蝠的冠状病毒的传播性并断言“明确”所需的风险效益审查HHS P3CO指南。
 NIH于2020年4月终止了Ecohealth Grant。在2020年6月的房屋能源和商务委员会之前，Fauci表示他不知道为什么赠款被取消！
 Covid-19可以在中国南部开发，以某种方式得到了武汉动物市场吗？首先，未知蝙蝠在武汉野生动物市场销售。其次，中国等效于我们的CDC测试41种，并迄今为止没有携带能够将病毒传递给人类病毒的中间物种。第三，武汉还有其他Covid-19人类案例，没有可追溯接触这个市场。如果没有识别的受感染的动物，它看起来更有可能武汉市场是一个超级展位事件而不是原产地。 
我认为最有可能的情景是WIV的实验室人员的污染，我们知道Coronavirus获得职能研究正在进行，并且有关于其安全的能力的严重问题。感染的实验室人员可以在2019年10月和11月展开人口中的病毒吗？我们可以通过在2019年12月在本疾病的第一个已知的疾病之前学习在中国收集的血液样本来获得该情景的更多证据。这显然需要中国政府的合作，我们迄今没有见过。
   中国政府在WIV实验室欠世界完全透明度。世界必须学习这个病毒的起源，这样它就在源于WIV实验室，我们可以采取必要的步骤，以确保进一步这样的病毒研究是安全的......或者可能禁止到处禁止它。
 Greg Ganske博士是一名退休的外科医生和代表大会的前议员，他们在能源和商务委员会雇用，司法管辖区是国家卫生研究院和疾病控制和预防的中心。
note_en:
The Chinese government owes the world complete transparency at the Wuhan lab. We need to know how to make virus research as safe as possible.
     We are now 16 months into the most serious global pandemic since the Spanish flu of 1919, and we still don’t know the origin of the virus. If we want to prevent the next epidemic, it is vital to find out. However, these efforts are mired in politics and incomplete information.
 In the critical first weeks of the pandemic, Beijing suppressed essential information and prevented World Health Organization investigators from entering the country. When a Chinese biologist courageously posted the sequenced genome of the virus online,  his lab was shut down for “rectification.”
  However, we don’t know exactly where in Wuhan the coronavirus that causes the illness came from, or how it became deadly and transmissible to humans. The virus’s closest relatives have been  collected in bats from Yunnan Province since 2013. COVID-19 was first reported in December 2019 in Wuhan city, which is more than 1,000 miles away from Yunnan. Dr. Anthony Fauci, director of the U.S. National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, has said the source was  most likely an animal “wet market” in Wuhan; Dr. Robert Redfield, former director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, has argued that the virus  most likely came from the Chinese Institute of Virology in Wuhan,  as has former Secretary of State Mike Pompeo.
  In a preliminary report, the World Health Organization reported that a lab leak was “extremely unlikely.” However, WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus cautioned that the team’s probe in the potential lab leak was  limited by the Chinese government and that further investigation was needed. The director-general wants to send specialists back to the  Chinese Institute of Virology, or WIV, to explore more completely the possibility of a lab leak origin.
   This is pertinent since  State Department cables from before the epidemic in 2018 cautioned that diplomatic experts who had visited the facility had concerns that proper safety measures were not being observed. One of the cables warned that the WIV, a  biosafety-4 lab, which is the highest-level biocontainment certification, was working on bat-based coronaviruses and that there was the risk of a new SARS-like pandemic because the researchers had shown that various SARS-like coronaviruses can interact with the human receptor (ACE2) identified for SARS-corona virus.  The State Department subsequently announced that researchers in the WIV lab reported flu-like symptoms  in the fall of 2019, months before the Chinese authorities acknowledged the  outbreak.
  It is indisputable that the Wuhan lab was doing bat coronavirus research. We know this because during the Obama administration,  Fauci authorized U.S. funding of WIV research on bat viruses to the tune of a nearly $700,000  National Institutes of Health grant in 2015. Specifically, Fauci allowed the grant to EcoHealth to genetically modify bat-based coronaviruses  to bypass the HHS Pandemic Pathogen Control and Oversight, or P3CO, review board, whose purpose it is to monitor virus research that could make viruses more contagious, what is known as gain-of-function research. Advocates say  such work can aid understanding of and predictions about dangerous emerging pathogens, among other benefits, but the obvious dangers make the research controversial.
  United States gain-of-function research  was suspended in 2014 after a  CDC anthrax exposure that came on the heels of a worldwide scientific outcry in 2011 when it was revealed that labs in Wisconsin and the Netherlands were  intentionally modifying H5N1 bird flu virus to more effectively jump species.
 Fauci outsourced viral research to China’s Wuhan lab and licensed the lab to continue receiving U.S. federal funding. The National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases has discretion in notifying the P3CO committee,  which was formed in 2017, of potentially dangerous research, and Fauci exercised that discretion by exempting from review the research grant to EcoHealth. NIAID says this was not gain-of-function research, but other scientists contest that and say it should have been reviewed by P3CO.
   A 2015 article in Nature Medicine magazine shared the conclusions of researchers who investigated a virus called SHC014 that is found in bats in China. They created a virus made up of a surface protein of SHC014 with the backbone of a SARS virus that had been adapted to grow in mice and to mimic human disease. This chimera infected human airway cells, proving that the surface protein of SHC014 had the necessary structure to bind a receptor on the human cells and to infect them.
  EcoHealth has a history of manipulating bat-based viruses. Its president, Peter Daszak, said this  in a podcast from Singapore shortly before the first reported cases of COVID-19 in December 2019: “You can manipulate them in the lab pretty easily. Spike protein drives a lot of what happens with the corona virus zoonotic risk. So you can get the sequence, you can build the protein … insert the backbone of another virus and do some work in the lab.”
  Rutgers University professor of chemical biology Richard H. Ebright thinks the infectious disease institute was wrong in determining that the EcoHealth grant did not involve enhancing the transmissibility of Chinese bat-based coronavirus and  asserts that the research ”unequivocally” required risk-benefit review under the HHS P3CO guidelines.
 The NIH  terminated the EcoHealth grant in April 2020. During a hearing before the House Energy and Commerce Committee in June, 2020,  Fauci said he didn’t know why the grant was canceled!
 Could COVID-19 have developed in southern China and somehow gotten to the Wuhan animal market?   First, bats were not known to have been sold in the Wuhan wildlife market. Second, the  Chinese equivalent of our CDC tested 41 species in the market and to date no intermediary species carrying the virus capable of transmitting the virus to humans has been identified.  Third, there were other COVID-19 human cases in Wuhan with no traceable contact with this market. Without an identified infected animal, it looks more likely the Wuhan market was a super-spreader event rather than the origin.
   I think the most likely scenario is a contamination of lab personnel at the WIV where we know coronavirus gain-of-function research was being done and where there were serious questions about its ability to do that research safely. Could infected lab personnel have then spread the virus in the population back in October and November of 2019?  We might gain more proof of that scenario by studying blood samples collected in China before the first known outbreak of the disease in December 2019.  This obviously would require the cooperation of the Chinese government, which we have not seen to date.
   The Chinese government owes the world complete transparency at the WIV lab. The world must learn the origin of this virus so that, if it did originate in the WIV laboratory, we can take the steps necessary to make sure further such virus research is safe … or possibly prohibit it everywhere.
 Dr. Greg Ganske is a retired surgeon and former member of Congress who served on the Energy and Commerce Committee, with jurisdiction of the National Institutes of Health and Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
title: 我们需要了解Covid-19的起源
title_en: We need to know the origin of Covid-19

一个空间年龄“公共悲剧”在我们的鼻子下方展开 - 或者真的，在我们的头脑上 - 而且没有达成共识，但如何阻止它。超过半个世纪的人，人类一直在越来越多地将物体投入低地轨道。随着少数发布进一步推动越来越拥挤的境界的有意义的局限性，普遍存在的态度一直持续宽容：在轨道上，似乎，总有一个空间。
 经过多十年的高速杂乱的堆积，以花火火箭阶段，流浪螺栓和涂料芯片，固体火箭电动机渣，死亡或垂死的卫星以及来自抗脱石的​​散射片段 - 所有这些都可以单独地进行损坏或摧毁其他资产 - 低地轨道终于濒临舒适的边缘。问题现在已经准备好，因为卫星“Mega Constellations”需要数千个航天器，例如Spacex的Starlink，宽带互联网网络。 Starlink是许多类似项目之一：来自一个名为OneWeb的公司的另一个Mega星座已经部署。亚马逊的项目Kuiper正在寻求在不久的将来创建高达3,200颗卫星的Mega星座。
 随着拥堵的发展，轨道资产之间也在近距离呼叫。例如，国际空间站定期调整其轨道以避免潜在的危险碎片。更糟糕的是，在威胁威胁威胁的威胁中，已经有一个更加威胁，这种侵略性拒绝会加剧已经糟糕的情况。考虑2009年2月的俄罗斯宇宙卫星和商业铱星航天之间的运行，这产生了巨大的碎片。
 奥尔巴尔德·斯巴塞（Orbital Debris Research）退休的NASS高级科学家唐纳德卡斯勒表示，寻找至少一些空间垃圾的方法应该是一个最高的全球优先级。在20世纪70年代后期，他预测了一种被称为Kessler综合征的情景的可能性：由于空间垃圾的密度增加，可以出现碎屑产生碰撞的级联，自我维持失控循环，这可能最终使低地轨道产生低地轨道太危险，无法支持大多数空间活动。
 凯斯勒说：“即使在社区内，碎片环境也达到了”折扣点“，其中碎片将继续增加，即使所有启动都停止，”Kessler说。 “它需要一个铱星型碰撞，以使每个人的注意力。这就是它归结为......我们逾期的东西会发生这种情况。“
 至于Kessler综合症，“它已经开始，”碎片专家说。 “碰撞发生了所有时间的戏剧性而不是大尺寸的尺度，”凯斯勒补充道。
  Kessler的噩梦情景没有可能缺乏可能的碎片冲洗修复：网，激光爆炸，麻疹，巨型泡沫球，空气，有事和太阳能风帆 - 以及垃圾收集机器人武器和触手 - 都已提出取出我们轨道垃圾的解决方案。 
通过这种令人担忧的事态努力努力的新参赛者是Astroscale示范（ELSA-D）使命刚刚推出了生活结束服务。 Elsa-D是由日本卫星服务公司Astroscale开发的双卫星任务：它包括一个“服务器”卫星，旨在安全地从轨道上删除碎片，并将其作为感兴趣的对象。该项目旨在展示一种磁力系统，可捕获稳定甚至翻滚物体，无论是处置还是在轨道上进行服务。在多相测试议程之后，服务器和客户端将在一起，在他们的火热进入地球的大气中崩埋。
 Elsa-D现在在地球轨道上盘旋。该特派团于3月22日通过俄罗斯Soyuz火箭队倾斜，将其他搭便车的卫星扔进太空。在升降机之后，Astrocale的创始人兼首席执行官诺布·奥卡达表示，Elsa-D将证明拆除碎片 - 去除能力和“推动监管开发，并推进生意和积极的碎片清除服务的商业案例”。他说，发射是实现“为未来几代利益的安全和可持续发展的空间发展”一步。
 虽然ELSA-D和其它ILK的其他技术示范对清除轨道碎片的毫无疑问的积极发展，但它们不应被误认为是治愈物。尽管他们的成功谦虚，但这些任务仍然缺乏解决手头的动态困境，而空间垃圾的扩散基本上是未公布的。
  “从我的角度来看，处理空间碎片的最佳解决方案不是首先产生它，”Celestrak的科学家T.S.Kelso说，一个分析团体，这是一种分析团体，它在地球轨道上留下了眼睛。 “就像任何环境问题一样，预防污染比以后清理污染更容易和更便宜。在完成任务后停止在轨道中留下轨道。“
 Kelso说，简单地没有“一定尺寸适合 - 所有解决方案”的空间垃圾问题。他观察到，去除大型火箭体比去除更多较小物体的相当质量，这是一个明显不同的任务。与此同时，SpaceX等公司的创新显着降低了发射成本，为远远超过卫星打开闸门，以达到低地轨道，有些人将不可避免地失败并变得漂移，杂物生成危险（除非他们被ELSA-D-除去）像空间拖船）。 “许多这些运营商开始了解继续躲避越来越多的碎片的难度和复杂性。”
 空间垃圾从纳米颗粒到整个航天器，如欧洲空间机构的envisat，这是一个双层巴士的大小，并在每个人的顶部和删除命中列表中的删除命中列表.Alice考古学家和空间垃圾说澳大利亚弗林德斯大学专家。
 还有其他物体，例如耐心重量，这是块状金属的固体块，以及纸薄的热毯。 “他们会导致不同类型的损害，可能需要不同的策略来删除。 “戈尔曼说，没有办法是一定尺寸的方法将要这样做。” 
她说，最严重的风险来自一个和10厘米的碎片颗粒。 “除了整个空间的宇宙飞船，碰撞概率远远超过了它们，”戈尔曼说。 “虽然碎片这种大小可能不会导致灾难性的分手，与之碰撞肯定会损害工作卫星并创造新的碎片颗粒。”
 让她注意卫星巨型星座，Gorman担心他们在已经拥挤的低地轨道环境中的影响。 “我们也知道轨道动态可能是不可预测的，”她说。 “我希望看到这些Mega星座运营商中的一些释放他们的长期建模，因为越来越多的卫星被推出。”
 Gorman说，毫无疑问，无疑的轨道碎片去除在技术上是挑战性的。 “然而，大问题是，任何可以去除现有碎片的成功技术也可以用作抗外露武器，”她说。 “这是一个完整的蠕虫，需要外交和谈判，最重要的是，在国际一级信任。”
 事实上，乔治州理工学院山姆国际事务学院副教授Mariel Borowitz近年来，近年来，康复到轨道和执行服务或破坏的能力对军事规划师来说，这是近年来的兴趣。 “这些迅速推进的技术有可能用于和平的空间活动或在太空中战争，”她说。 “鉴于他们的能力的双重使用性质，我必须提前知道他们将如何在任何一天使用。”
  目前，由于德克萨斯大学奥斯汀大学的奥尔巴哈·耶和华奥尔巴·耶和华德克萨斯州的奥尔比斯·耶利拉专家，不可批准的空间碎片拆除的商业案例，而且比真正的市场更具“PowerPoint谈话”。
 “我认为人们希望政府基本来到一些常识，帮助创造和建立一个互动的行业市场，”贾说。为了使这种情况发生，他认为，太空航空公司必须同意近地球空间是一种像土地，空气和海洋的生态系统。 “这不是无限的，所以我们需要环保，”他说。
 JAH在思想空间可持续发展度量，类似于碳足迹。 “让我们称之为”空间流量“足迹，”他说。 “我们需要一种方式，我们可以量化”轨道高速公路“饱和交通饱和，使其无法使用。然后，您可以为对象分配赏金并讨论非呼叫碎片删除。也许对他们死亡资产的主权所有者占据了轨道的能力，这可能会受到惩罚。这绝对可以创建一个空间 - 拆卸技术可以茁壮成长的市场。“ 
还需要用于空间中的对象的分类方案。 Jah说，拥有如此的分类法，将有助于解决除了轨道杂乱的不同群体所需的技术类型类型。  至于大局，Jah表示这是一个简单的数字游戏：发射速率超过了重新进入地球大气的空间物体的速率。 “那不是一种很大的能量平衡，”他补充道。  哈拉斯，贾说，政策制定者仍然在对这个问题的反应中缓慢。 毕竟，虽然2009年宇宙 - 铱碰撞等事件产生了大量的碎片，但它们仍然非常罕见。  “在我看来，2009年的碰撞相当于泰坦尼克号的乘客，从冰山中磕磕碰碰，然后在甲板上玩耍，”贾说。 “就危险的轨道碎片而言，事情已经是一种不利的方式，因为我们没有改变我们的行为。”
note_en:
A Space Age “tragedy of the commons” is unfolding right under our nose—or, really, right over our head—and no consensus yet exists on how to stop it. For more than a half-century, humans have been hurling objects into low-Earth orbit in ever growing numbers. And with few meaningful limitations on further launches into that increasingly congested realm, the prevailing attitude has been persistently permissive: in orbit, it seems, there is always room for one more.
 After so many decades of the buildup of high-speed clutter in the form of spent rocket stages, stray bolts and paint chips, solid-rocket-motor slag, dead or dying satellites and the scattered fragments from antisatellite tests—all of which could individually damage or destroy other assets—low-Earth orbit is finally on the verge of becoming too crowded for comfort. And the problem is now poised to get much worse because of the rise of satellite “mega constellations” requiring thousands of spacecraft, such as SpaceX’s Starlink, a broadband Internet network. Starlink is but one of many similar projects: Another mega constellation from a company called OneWeb is already being deployed. And Amazon’s Project Kuiper is seeking to create a mega constellation of up to 3,200 satellites in the near future.
 As the congestion has grown, so too have close calls between orbiting assets. The International Space Station, for instance, regularly tweaks its orbit to avoid potentially hazardous debris. Worse yet, there has been an uptick in the threat of full-on collisions that generate menacing refuse that exacerbates the already bad situation. Consider the February 2009 run-in between a dead Russian Cosmos satellite and a commercial Iridium spacecraft, which produced an enormous amount of debris.
 Finding ways to remove at least some of all that space junk should be a top global priority, says Donald Kessler, a retired NASA senior scientist for orbital debris research. In the late 1970s he foretold the possibility of a scenario that has been dubbed the Kessler syndrome: as the density of space rubbish increases, a cascading, self-sustaining runaway cycle of debris-generating collisions can arise that might ultimately make low-Earth orbit too hazardous to support most space activities.
 “There is now agreement within the community that the debris environment has reached a ‘tipping point’ where debris would continue to increase even if all launches were stopped,” Kessler says. “It takes an Iridium-Cosmos-type collision to get everyone’s attention. That’s what it boils down to.... And we’re overdue for something like that to happen.”
 As for the Kessler syndrome, “it has already started,” the debris expert says. “There are collisions taking place all the time—less dramatic and not at the large size scale,” Kessler adds.
  Kessler’s nightmare scenario has yielded no shortage of possible debris-flushing fixes: nets, laser blasts, harpoons, giant foam balls, puffs of air, tethers and solar sails—as well as garbage-gathering robotic arms and tentacles—have all been proposed as solutions for taking out our orbital trash.
 A new entrant in grappling with this worrisome state of affairs is the just launched End-of-Life Services by Astroscale Demonstration (ELSA-d) mission. ELSA-d is a two-satellite mission developed by Astroscale, a Japan-based satellite services company: it consists of a “servicer” satellite designed to safely remove debris from orbit and a “client” one that doubles as an object of interest. The project aims to showcase a magnetic system that can capture stable and even tumbling objects, whether for disposal or servicing in orbit. Following a multiphase test agenda, the servicer and client will then deorbit together, disintegrating during their fiery plunge into Earth’s atmosphere.
 ELSA-d is now circling in Earth orbit. The mission was lofted on March 22 via a Russian Soyuz rocket that tossed scads of other hitchhiking satellites into space. Following the liftoff, Astroscale’s founder and CEO Nobu Okada said  ELSA-d will prove out debris-removal capabilities and “propel regulatory developments and advance the business case for end-of-life and active debris removal services.” The launch is a step toward realizing “safe and sustainable development of space for the benefit of future generations,” he said.
 Although ELSA-d and other technology demonstrations of its ilk are unquestionably positive developments for clearing orbital debris, they should not be mistaken for cure-alls. Despite their modest successes, such missions are falling short of addressing the dynamic dilemma at hand, and the proliferation of space junk continues essentially unabated.
  “From my perspective, the best solution to dealing with space debris is not to generate it in the first place,” says T. S. Kelso, a scientist at CelesTrak, an analytic group that keeps an eye on Earth-orbiting objects. “Like any environmental issue, it is easier and far less expensive to prevent pollution than to clean it up later. Stop leaving things in orbit after they have completed their mission.”
 There simply is no “one-size-fits-all solution” to the problem of space junk, Kelso says. Removing large rocket bodies is a significantly different task than removing the equivalent mass of a lot more smaller objects, which are in a wide range of orbits, he observes. Meanwhile innovations by companies such as SpaceX are dramatically lowering launch costs, opening the floodgates for far more satellites to reach low-Earth orbit, where some will inevitably fail and become drifting, debris-generating hazards (unless they are removed by ELSA-d-like space tugs). “Many of these operators are starting to understand the difficulty and complexity of continuing to dodge the growing number of debris.”
 Space junk ranges from nanoparticles to whole spacecraft such as the European Space Agency’s Envisat, which is the size of a double-decker bus and at the top of everyone's removal hit list, says Alice Gorman, a space archaeologist and space junk expert at Flinders University in Australia.
 There are also objects such as despin weights, which are solid lumps of metal, and thermal blankets, which are paper-thin. “They’ll cause different types of damage and may need different strategies to remove. There is no way that a one-size-fits-all approach is going to do it,” Gorman says .
 The most serious risks, she says, come from debris particles between one and 10 centimeters in size. “There’s far more of them than whole defunct spacecraft, and there is a far greater probability of collision,” Gorman says. “While debris this size might not cause a catastrophic breakup, collision with it can certainly damage working satellites and create new debris particles.”
 Turning her attention to satellite mega constellations, Gorman worries about their effects in a low-Earth orbital environment that is already congested. “We also know that orbital dynamics can be unpredictable,” she says. “I want to see some of these mega constellation operators releasing their long-term modeling for collisions as more and more satellites are launched.”
 There is no doubt that active orbital debris removal is technically challenging, Gorman says. “However, the big issue is that any successful technology that can remove an existing piece of debris can also be used as an antisatellite weapon,” she says. “This is a whole other can of worms that requires diplomacy and negotiation and, most importantly, trust at the international level.”
 Indeed, the ability to cozy up to spacecraft in orbit and perform servicing or sabotage has spurred considerable interest from military planners in recent years, says Mariel Borowitz, an associate professor at the Georgia Institute of Technology’s Sam Nunn School of International Affairs. “These rapidly advancing technologies have the potential to be used for peaceful space activities or for warfare in space,” she says. “Given the dual-use nature of their capabilities, it’s impossible to know for sure in advance how they’ll be used on any given day.”
  For now, according to Moriba Jah, an orbital debris expert at the University of Texas at Austin, the business case for space debris removal is not monetizable and is more a “PowerPoint talk” than a real marketplace.
 “I think people are hoping that government basically comes to some common sense to help create and establish a marketplace for industries to engage in these sorts of activities,” Jah says. In order for that to happen, he believes that spacefaring nations have to agree that near-Earth space is an ecosystem like land, air and the ocean. “It’s not infinite, so we need environmental protection,” he says.
 Jah has in mind space sustainability metrics akin to a carbon footprint. “Let’s call it a ‘space traffic’ footprint,” he says. “We need a way we can quantify at what point an ‘orbital highway’ gets saturated with traffic so that it’s not usable. Then you can assign a bounty for objects and talk about nonconsensual debris removal. Maybe there is a penalty to the sovereign owner of their dead asset that’s taking up capacity of an orbit. This could definitely create a marketplace where space-object-removal technologies can thrive.”
 A classification scheme for objects in space is also needed. Having such a taxonomy, Jah says, would help sort out what types of technologies are required for removing different pedigrees of orbital clutter.
 As for the big picture, Jah says it is a simple numbers game: the rate of launches exceeds the rate of space objects reentering Earth’s atmosphere. “That’s not a great kind of energy balance,” he adds.
 Alas, Jah says, policy makers are still sluggish in their reactions to the problem. After all, although events such as the 2009 Cosmos-Iridium collision generate massive amounts of debris, they are still quite rare—for now.
 “In my view, that 2009 collision was equivalent to passengers on the  Titanic feeling that bump from an iceberg, and then there’s a band playing on deck,” Jah says. “In terms of hazardous orbital debris, things are already going a detrimental way because we haven’t changed our behavior.”
title: 空间垃圾拆卸不会顺利
title_en: Space Junk Removal Is Not Going Smoothly

note_en:
Security researchers have uncovered a batch of Google Play apps that stole users’ text messages and made unauthorized purchases on users’ dime.
  The malware, which was hidden in eight apps that had more than 700,000 downloads, hijacked SMS message notifications and then made unauthorized purchases, McAfee mobile researchers Sang Ryol Ryu and Chanung Pak  said Monday. McAfee is calling the malware Android/Etinu.  The researchers said an investigation of the attacker-operated server that controlled infected devices showed it stored all kinds of date from users' phones, including their mobile carrier, phone number, SMS messages, IP address, country, and network status. The server also stored auto-renewing subscriptions, some of which looked like this:
    The malware is reminiscent, if not identical, to a prolific family of Android malware known as Joker, which  also steals SMS messages and signs up users for pricey services. “The malware hijacks the Notification Listener to steal incoming SMS messages like Android Joker malware does, without the SMS read permission,” the researchers wrote referring to Etinu. “Like a chain system, the malware then passes the notification object to the final stage. When the notification has arisen from the default SMS package, the message is finally sent out using WebView JavaScript Interface.”
 While the researchers say that Etinu is a malware family distinct from Joker, security software from Microsoft, Sophos, and other companies use the word Joker in their detection names of some of the newly discovered malicious apps. Etinu’s decryption flow and use of multi-stage payloads are also similar.
     In an email, McAfee’s Sang Ryol Ryu wrote: “While Etinu looks very similar to Joker, in-depth, its processes for loading payloads, encryption, targeting geographies are different from Joker.”
 The Etinu payloads appear in an Android Assets folder with file names such as “cache.bin,” “settings.bin,” “data.droid,” or "image files."
   As depicted in the decryption flow diagram above, hidden malicious code in the main installation file downloaded from Play opens an encrypted file named “1.png” and decrypts it using a key that’s the same as the package name. The resulting file, “loader.dex” is then executed, resulting in an HTTP POST request to the C2 server.
 “Interestingly, this malware uses key management servers,” the McAfee researchers wrote. “It requests keys from the servers for the AES encrypted second payload, ‘2.png.’ And the server returns the key as the ‘s’ value of JSON. Also, this malware has self-update function. When the server responds ‘URL’ value, the content in the URL is used instead of ‘2.png’. However, servers do not always respond to the request or return the secret key.”

这是安全行为通过代表房屋的第四次。以前的账单版本在2019年通过了这所房子，但后来在参议院委员会去世，从未达到过时的特朗普桌子。
 根据本条例草案，安全法案将在与符合国家州法律的大麻公司经营时，保护银行和信用工会免受联邦起诉。这将开辟金融机构的传统资本线，这一直无法获得，因为大麻仍然在联邦一级被认为是非法的。安全法案将允许这些银行与大麻是合法的国家的运营商合作。
  大麻目前在三十六个州，四个地区和哥伦比亚区的医学目的是合法的。娱乐用途是十八个国家，两个领土和D.C的成年人合法的。
 该法案通过了广泛的Bipartisan支持，并批准了321-101的房子。在该法案的通过之前，一封显示立法的信函被派遣了从20个国家州长和来自每个州的一个美国领土和银行家的领导，以及国家财务主管联盟。
 通过双方的支持，倡导者和行业专家们感到更有信心，这条例草案将通过参议院并达到拜登总统的办公桌。
note_en:
This is the fourth time the SAFE ACT passed the House of Representatives. A previous version of the bill passed the House in 2019, but later died in a Senate committee and never reached then-President Trump’s desk.
 Under the current bill, the SAFE ACT would protect banks and credit unions from federal prosecution when operating with cannabis companies compliant with their state’s laws. This would open up traditional lines of capital from financial institutions, which have been unavailable since cannabis is still considered illegal on a federal level. The SAFE ACT would allow these banks to work with operators in states where cannabis is legal.
  Cannabis is currently legal for medical purposes in thirty-six states, four territories, and the District of Columbia. Recreational use is legal for adults in eighteen states, two territories, and D.C.
 The bill passed the House with broad bipartisan support and was approved by 321-101. Before the bill’s passing, a letter showing support of the legislation was sent to House leadership from 20 state governors and one U.S. Territory and bankers from each state and a coalition of state treasurers.
 With support from both parties, advocates and industry experts feel more confident that this bill will pass the Senate and reach President Biden’s desk.
title: 大麻银行法案通过Bipartisan支持，美国房子
title_en: Cannabis banking act passes U.S. House with bipartisan support

note:
近十年前成立，章鱼部署已成长为服务于25,000个组织，包括Microsoft，NASA，Xero，Disney和Stack Overflow，通过自引导。今天，该公司宣布了第一个外界投资。 Insight Partners在八达通股权部署了1.725亿美元，并有助于自动化企业软件部署公司开始下一阶段的增长。
 在创始人兼首席执行官Paul Stovell观察到章鱼部署，观察到许多软件团队能够建立连续集成（CI）服务器，但努力实现全面连续部署（CD）。 Stovell和他的妻子Sonia Stovell，章鱼部署的首席财务官，开始在2011年致力于该公司作为一个“夜间和周末”项目。到明年，它已达到盈利能力。现在章鱼部署在澳大利亚，美国和英国雇用了100多人。
  Insight Partners与软件公司合作，扩大运营。其他投资组合企业在其扩展网络中包括多人之城，购物，推特，平静和高音乐。章鱼部署计划利用其投资来增加企业市场份额，特别是在美国。
  Stovell在电子邮件中告诉TechCrunch，即章鱼部署“拒绝的风险投资方法，并且已经转向VC公司多年。然而，我们待的增长途径意味着在某些时候，带来投资者可能有意义。“
 “我们最大的客户分部是企业，并在考虑后，我们决定将投资者纳入投资者的正确步骤，了解企业上市，缩放公司，以及理解下一个5-的合作伙伴10年的增长将看起来像章鱼，“他补充道。
 在新闻陈述中，洞察力合作伙伴董事迈克尔Triplett表示，“我们经常与我们的投资组合公司交谈有关他们使用的产品或者客户正在使用的产品和章鱼部署一遍又一遍地提出。公司在雷达下飞行，但是当你与客户交谈时，他们是巨大的粉丝。对我们来说，章鱼是企业部署自动化的领导者。“
note_en:
Founded almost a decade ago,  Octopus Deploy has grown to serve 25,000 organizations, including Microsoft, NASA, Xero, Disney and Stack Overflow, through bootstrapping. Today the company announced its first outside investment. Insight Partners has taken a minority stake in Octopus Deploy for $172.5 million and will help the automated enterprise software deployment company embark on its next stage of growth.
 Octopus Deploy was launched after founder and chief executive officer Paul Stovell observed that many software teams were able to set up continuous integration (CI) servers, but struggled to achieve full continuous deployment (CD). Stovell and his wife Sonia  Stovell, Octopus Deploy’s chief financial officer, began working on the company as a “nights-and-weekend” project in 2011. By the next year, it had reached profitability. Now Octopus Deploy employs more than 100 people in Australia, the United States and the United Kingdom.
  Insight Partners works with software companies to scale up their operations. Other portfolio companies in its ScaleUp Network include Pluralsight, Shopify, Twitter, Calm and Qualtrics. Octopus Deploy plans to use its investment to increase its enterprise market share, especially in the United States.
  Stovell told TechCrunch in an email that Octopus Deploy has “declined venture approaches and have been turning away VC firms for years. However, the growth path we’ve been on meant that at some point, it might make sense to bring on an investor.”
 “Our largest customer segment was the enterprise, and after consideration, we decided it was the right step to bring on board an investor that understood enterprise go-to-market, scaling up a company, and a partner that understood what the next 5-10 years of growth will look like for Octopus,” he added.
 In a press statement, Insight Partners managing director Michael Triplett said, “We routinely talk to our portfolio companies about the products they use or that their customers are using and Octopus Deploy came up over and over. The company has flown under the radar, but when you talk to their customers, they are huge fans. It is clear to us that Octopus is the leader in enterprise deployment automation.”
title: 在十年后，章鱼部署从Insight Partners筹集了172.5米
title_en: After a decade of bootstrapping, Octopus Deploy raises $172.5M from Insight Partners

新版本包括从Google云的支持范围作为基础架构提供商（现在的预览）到新的自动化API，该功能将Pulumi转换为可以从其他应用程序调用的库中。它基本上允许开发人员编写工具，例如，可以为SaaS应用程序的每个客户提供和配置自己的基础架构。
   该公司还在启动Pulumi封装和组件，以便创建开发人员可以从他们的首选语言调出开发人员的自由基础架构构建块。
 还有新的是支持所有公司的支付计划的Pulumi的CI / CD助理。此功能使得更容易通过十几个流行的CI / CD平台部署云基础架构和应用程序，包括AWS代码服务，Azure Devops，Circleci，Gitlab CI，Google Cloud Build，Jenkins，Travis CI和Spinnaker。到目前为止，您需要参加团队专业或企业计划来使用它，但现在可以使用所有支付用户。
 此外，该公司还在扩展其一些企业功能，例如SAML SSO，SCIM同步和新角色类型。
 “当我们在普鲁米开始时，我们知道我们希望开发商和基础设施团队能够更接近地建立更具创新的软件，”普鲁迪夫，Pulumi联合创始人兼首席执行官说。 “我们尚未知道的是，我们最终会致电这个”云工程“，我们的客户也称之为，他们也会与我们一起旅行。我们现在将我们的整个平台围绕这一核心想法，现在随着现代云继续破坏整个业务模式，这是现在加速的核心想法。 Pulumi 3.0是实现未来的这种愿景的令人兴奋的里程碑 - 民主化对云的访问和帮助球队在一起建立更好的软件 - 更多来提出。“
note_en:
The new release includes features that range from support for Google Cloud as an infrastructure provider (now in preview) to a new Automation API that turns Pulumi into a library that can then be called from other applications. It basically allows developers to write tools that, for example, can then provision and configure their own infrastructure for each customer of a SaaS application, for example.
   The company is also launching Pulumi Packages and Components for creating opinionated infrastructure building blocks that developers can then call up from their preferred languages.
 Also new is support for Pulumi’s CI/CD Assistant across all the company’s paid plans. This feature makes it easier to deploy cloud infrastructure and applications through more than a dozen popular CI/CD platforms, including the likes of AWS Code Service, Azure DevOps, CircleCI, GitLab CI, Google Cloud Build, Jenkins, Travis CI and Spinnaker. Until now, you needed to be on a Team Pro or Enterprise plan to use this, but it’s now available to all paying users.
 In addition, the company is expanding some of its enterprise features with, for example, SAML SSO, SCIm synchronization and new role types.
 “When we started out on Pulumi, we knew we wanted to enable developers and infrastructure teams to collaborate more closely to build more innovative software,” said Joe Duffy, Pulumi co-founder and CEO. “What we didn’t know yet is that we’d end up calling this ‘Cloud Engineering,’ that our customers would call it that too, and that they would go on this journey with us. We are now centering our entire platform around this core idea which is now accelerating as the modern cloud continues to disrupt entire business models. Pulumi 3.0 is an exciting milestone in realizing this vision of the future — democratizing access to the cloud and helping teams build better software together — with much more to come.”
title: Pulumi推出其基础架构 - 代码平台的3.0版
title_en: Pulumi launches version 3.0 of its infrastructure-as-code platform

 社区银行和信贷工会占所有银行机构的一大百分比，这意味着Mantl的市场机会很大。金融机构在2020年增加了213％，因为金融机构克服了在Covid-19大流行后消费者对消费者的数字产品的需求增加。
  今天，该公司宣布它已经筹集了4000万美元的一系列由字母的独立增长基金，首页领导的B系列资金，以帮助它增长更多。融资自成立以6070万美元以来，融资将提出的总资金提出，并包括参与D1资本合作伙伴，BoxGroup和现有的支持者Point72企业，ClockTower技术企业和Oldslip组。该公司去年7月筹集了1900万美元，在该年4月的储存量增长705％后。

 该公司表示，这种过时的技术使社区银行和信贷工会等许多金融机构（如社区银行和信贷工会）限制了消费者提供的数字银行期权。
  因此，该公司根据大多数社区银行和信贷工会对维持美国金融体系的竞争和公平至关重要的前提。
 “在高水平，Mantl是一家企业软件公司，真正专注于帮助传统的金融机构现代化和成长，”哈雷告诉TechCrunch。 “我们在一天结束时的使命是通过涉及遗留基础设施来真正扩大对金融服务的访问，这确实阻碍了数字银行的访问权限。” 
该公司声称，其白标本开放软件允许银行和客户“在任何时候从任何地方开立账户，在不到三分钟的任何设备上。”

 “银行自然是非常冒险的厌恶，我们需要建立，以便充分承担他们在工作的完整基础设施，”哈雷说。 “账户开放风险低，但考虑到不到50％的银行实际上有在线帐户开幕，这也非常高。”
 Mantl直接集成到遗产基础设施中，也称为核心银行系统，以加强该系统，帮助机构快速启动数码产品。

 由于首页审查了帮助银行有数字化转型的公司的景观，德珀斯出来，由于其“用户体验，导致所有尺寸的银行的存款增长和数量的价值。”

 自2013年成立以来，首都投资了一些金融金，包括MX，条纹，罗布尼，信用卡，艾伯特，Aye财经和贷款公司。
note_en:
Enter   Mantl , a startup that has developed technology to make it easier for people to open accounts digitally at community banks and credit unions so that those institutions can increase deposits and ultimately, profits. Founded in 2016, New York-based Mantl has been described by some as “the Shopify of account opening.”
 Community banks and credit unions make up a big percentage of all banking institutions, which means Mantl’s market opportunity is pretty darn large. The fintech’s revenue increased by 213% in 2020 as financial institutions clamored to meet increased demand for digital offerings from consumers in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic.
  And today, the company is announcing it has raised $40 million in a Series B round of funding led by Alphabet’s independent growth fund, CapitalG, to help it grow even more. The financing brings Mantl’s total funding raised since inception to $60.7 million and included participation from D1 Capital Partners, BoxGroup and existing backers Point72 Ventures, Clocktower Technology Ventures and OldSlip Group. The company  raised $19 million last July after growing deposit volume by 705% in April of that year.
  Mantl originally set out to build its own challenger bank, but in doing so realized there are 10,000 banks and credit unions in the U.S., and that 96% of them outsourced their technology to third-party legacy vendors such as Fiserv and Jack Henry, many of which have technology that is in some cases “decades old,” according to Nathaniel Harley, co-founder and CEO at Mantl.
 Such outdated technology has kept many financial institutions such as community banks and credit unions from competing online, and also limits the digital banking options available to consumers, the company said.
  So the company pivoted, based on the premise that most community banks and credit unions are critical to maintaining competition and equity in the United States’ financial system.
 “At a high level, Mantl is an enterprise software company that is really focused on helping traditional financial institutions modernize and grow,” Harley told TechCrunch. “Our mission at the end of the day is to really expand the access to financial services by taking on the legacy infrastructure, which has really hindered access to digital banking.”
 The company claims that its white-labeled account opening software allows banks and customers “to open an account from anywhere at any time, on any device in less than three minutes.”
 Th rough its flagship account opening software, Mantl claims to have helped community institutions — many of which are competing online for the first time — establish efficient   and  profitable digital operations. Among the community banks it works with are  Cross River Bank ,  Quontic  and  Midwest BankCentre .
 “Banks are naturally very risk averse, and we need to build in order to fully take on that full infrastructure that they’re working in,” Harley said. “Account opening is low risk, but it’s also extremely high value considering that less than 50% of banks actually have online account opening today.”
 Mantl integrates directly into the legacy infrastructure, also known as a core banking system, in order to enhance that system and help institutions launch digital products quickly.
 The company says its software also automates application decisioning for over 90% of cases while also reducing fraud by more than 60%.  This results in deposit growth that’s “typically 4x faster than other solutions on the market and up to 10x more cost-effective than building a new branch,” the company said.
 Combined, the institutions it works with have onboarded hundreds of thousands of new customers and raised billions of dollars in core deposits, the company claims.
 “We’re challenging the legacy infrastructure that is holding community institutions back,” Harley said,” and we see account opening as just the beginning.”
 The startup plans to use its new capital to do some hiring and expand its product offerings, including software that it says would be able to improve and digitize the onboarding experience for not just financial institutions but businesses of all sizes, from sole proprietors to complex commercial enterprises.
  CapitalG partner Jesse Wedler shares Mantl’s belief that banks form the backbone of this nation’s economy, both on a local and national level.
 “ While digitization has long been a priority for banks, it has become an urgent imperative as branches close and digital disruptors grow,” he said.
 As CapitalG reviewed the landscape of companies helping banks with digital transformation, Mantl stood out, Wedler said, due to its “user experience, resulting deposit growth and time-to-value for banks of all sizes.”
 But what has his firm most excited, he added, is the team’s vision for “transforming adjacent core banking applications.”
 Since its founding in 2013, CapitalG has invested in a number of fintechs, including MX,  Stripe, Robinhood, Credit Karma, Albert, Aye Finance and LendingClub.
title: 字母表的首页在Fintech Mantl领先4000万美元
title_en: Alphabet’s CapitalG leads $40 million round in fintech Mantl

note:
纽约市曾经是新的交通模式的早期采用者。在十八六十年代末，纽约人占据了速度，这是自行车的原始版本。半个世纪以后，这个城市拥有汽车，最终为化石燃料机器提供免费停车场 - 这是许多无类漫无无意的公民想要回来的昂贵公共空间的卓越赠品。纽约还在地上和地下建造并建造了地铁系统，在-19大流行袭击之前，每日举行五百五百万骑车者 - 一个美国人民的标志性，这座城市可能再也不会到达，如果遥远的工作是留下来。
 但是当谈到共享电动滑板车 - 成人的“踢”踏板的成人，电动版本，你用一只脚纽约推动了慢车道。与其自行车股份计划一样，花旗自行车，2013年推出的，纽约大多数其他大城市之后，纽约采用了保守的方法，以此在镇上的镇上的新模式。
  从加州南部，鸟类，后来，石灰，既有风险资本支持的科技创业公司，在圣莫尼卡的街道上落在Santa Monica的街道上，2017年出现了鸟类的车辆，石灰队的第一个城市2018年。绕过市政监管机构，公司希望尽快吸引客户。在优步国际成长之下，Travis Vanderzanden，Bird将其黑白滑板车纳入全球的一大十个城市，在一年一度的Blitzkrieg。盲目的城市政府，努力回应这座境内，暂时禁止西雅图，西好莱坞和温斯顿塞勒姆的踏板车。
  虽然鸟并不接近有利可图，但它很快就达到了独角兽地位 - 十亿美元的估价。然后石灰在独角兽围场加入鸟。投资者全部进入“微生物” - 嗡嗡声，捕获的术语，用于自行车和轻质电动车的术语，希望偶然进入下一个优步。在一年内，世界各地都会出现超过三十台式股票的初创公司。
 在许多城市中，踏板车共享比自行车分享更快，并通过更广泛的乘客人口。鸟类在前12个月内积累了超过一百万的游乐设施。用户喜欢踏板车的便利。例如，在奥斯汀，例如，在西南南部，踏板车被证明是跳跃在场地之间的理想选择。在西海岸，威尼斯海滩嘶嘶作着滑板车的声音。到2019年，长颈，平底机器已成为巴黎，维也纳，马德里和墨西哥城城市景观的夹具，如人行道上的机械天鹅聚类。
 运输人数被誉为踏板车分享作为他们的“最后一英里问题”的最佳解决方案，当火车站和家之间的旅行比步行距离 - 大多数距离大多数距离大多数人。未来主义将其视为第一个在其核心设计中将移动计算和全球定位技术纳入其中的第一运输方式，并将电子踏板车吹捧为未来电池供电的软件控制汽车的预兆。但对批评者E-Scooters是一个时尚，摩托车分享计划是一种技术喧嚣，用于利用有限的公共资源城市街道 - 丰富私人投资者。
  鸟和石灰吸引了受伤的车手的诉讼，并且来自遇到在人行道中间留下的笨拙的踏板车的很多人中热情的敌意。于2018年5月，旧金山接受了近两千名投诉后，向鸟，石灰和第三次运营商发出了停止和停止订单，旋转于2018年11月的福特购买。一级行动套装洛杉矶同年指责石灰，鸟类等“劝告和教唆袭击”。滑板车破坏成为表演艺术。 Instagram地点鸟墓地记录了破坏的鸟类，垃圾鸟类，海湾鸟类，以及鸟类小鸟。 
在西方的大型交通枢纽中，只有纽约和伦敦在现在被视为滑板车狂热的狂野阶段的野外阶段。然后来到全球的大流行，争抢的运输习惯，为运输理论家指的是“模式变化”的罕见机会。要从纽约越来越拥挤的自行车道判断，踏板模式已经到来。
 电子踏板车不是第一个试图进入纽约的运输系统的常设电动汽车。 Segway是一家两轮“人体运输车”，于2001年12月发布，并由Jeff Bezos炒作是“最着名和最着名的产品推出之一。”有贝佐斯和Segway的发明家迪恩卡曼有照片，骑在时代广场周边人行道上的机器。
 今天，人类的运输车可能是最好的记忆，因为保罗Blate在“商城警察”中的电子骏马，这是一个2009薄膜，这在妇女随便常设人的视野Gag倾斜。但是，SEGWAY的影响在广泛的国家和城市法律中徘徊，这些法律禁止了街道和人行道的大多数形式的单人E.V.S，包括电子骑自行车和电子摩托车。
  在二千岁的时候，第一波E-Bikes抵达城市作为食品送货工人，几乎所有移民，开始使用它们。对于一辆高级骑自行车的一笔百多美元的投资，一名工人可以每小时增加2美元的夜间收入 - 这可能在年度收益中达到数千美元。一些Yuppie早期的采用者也被带到了非法的自行车：我嫂子的肘部在曼哈顿打破了2010年，由一位电子骑自行车的电影制片人在自行车道上出了错误的方式。
 镇压在2017年开始，在六十岁的上游投资银行家，Matthew Shefler在哥伦布大道自行车道时钟骑自行车者中使用了速度枪，呼吁“询问市长”，在WNYC的“ Brian Lehrer表演，“并将现代Valocipedes的危险减少到比尔德布拉西奥。
  次年，n.y.p.d.发行送货工人数百个五百美元的引文，有时会带走他们的电子骑自行车。谈论中文或西班牙语的工人让他们的自行车被没收的比较高于英语的人被没收。司法联盟随着有影响力的当地政客，包括杰西卡拉莫斯（包括Brooklyn）的国家议会成员，包括杰西卡拉莫斯，包括杰西卡拉莫斯，以及来自布鲁克林的一个城市议会的Carlos Menchaca，但他们缺乏有效地在奥尔巴尼的大厅国家立法者的资金。踏板辅助电子自行车的地位最终澄清，因为豁免法律 - 花旗自行车开始在2018年开始电气舰队 - 但该市四万送货工人青睐的全新油门E-骑自行车仍然是非法的。
 在同一时期，米科西州公司开始关注纽约市场的利润丰厚，尽管被赛格威法律被阻止。鸟和石灰确实有资金在游说奥尔巴尼立法者上花费。鸟带来了布拉德利托斯，他们在二十多岁的时候设计了Uber扰乱了纽约市的战略; 2019年1月至6月在2019年之间，托斯送达了十万美元。石灰也在很大程度上花在游说中。 
菲尔·琼斯（Lime）的政府关系高级总监Phil Jones在制定踏板车公司的新法律方面发挥了主导作用。 “有很多总体州法律就入到底是由SEGWAY启发的两轮电动汽车违法，”他告诉我。 “这就是我们反对的东西，这就是交付工人的反对。”琼斯帮助巩固了五项旨在使两轮联合的E.V.S合法化为由Jessica Ramos赞助的参议院比尔5294A的一块立法。随着踏板车兄弟的金融资本和受迫害的送货扣的政治资本，该法案于2019年通过纽约大会通过，但总督安德鲁库米否决了它，表面上，因为他想要所有电子摩托车和电子摩托车骑手戴头盔。据企业称，潜在的原因是州长对国家政治中崛起的明星的敌对敌意。
  在2020年3月20日，Cuomo将状态放入锁定状态。几周内，N.Y.P.D的食品送货员一直骚扰被誉为前线英雄。在可怕的早期期间，特别是，似乎虽然救护车和送货电子骑自行车是唯一的车辆。只要骑车者年龄超过十八岁，就已经支持他对头盔的需求的Cuomo，签署了4月份的法案。
 7月，市议会强制着一名踏板车共享飞行员。 10月份，交通部发布了对利息表达的请求; d.o.t.将授予三个合同的申请人。纽约是世界上最大的潜在市场之一，对该概念来说是一个宝贵的证明地面，因此踏板车飞行员吸引了数十家公司的强烈兴趣 - 不仅是鸟和石灰，而且许多希望普遍存在的较小的运营商两个歌雨症。其中之一是链接，由超市，一个基于马萨诸塞州剑桥的工程和机器人公司所拥有的。 Link的荧光黄摩托车队队可以租用西雅图，奥克兰，马德里，罗马和17个其他城市。
 到2021年1月，纽约试点比赛的领域缩小为七。在布鲁克林海军院子的北极日，邀请决赛选手进行调整他们的滑板车和操作软件。观察者。
 “我们肯定是这里的失败者，”保罗稳定的白色超级节目的政策总监说，他瞥了一眼，他瞥了一眼，这些公司在那些正在在寒冷的寒战上设立滑板车的其他公司的代表。
 鸟和石灰也在海军院子里，也是Lyft，Ride-Haileing和Bike-Share Beimoth，这是花旗式自行车的舰队物流;福特的旋转; VOI是一家在欧洲城市运营的瑞典公司，自行车友好的基础设施使踏板踏板比在纽约的艰巨; Beryl，英国公司;和Veo，基于芝加哥的启动。
 怀特指出，在2020年9月，在西雅图的竞标中，白人队的胜利;链接是已发出许可证的三个运营商之一。 （石灰和轮子是一个混合枪手自行车的加利福尼亚州的制造商，接到了其他人。）白色归属鸟类对其破坏性历史的失败，以及公司使用演出劳动力为其踏板车提供服务。他希望这一联系，它在其运营的二十一城市拥有完善的合规性记录，并且从未使用过Gig劳动，将在纽约的监管机构中对鸟类进行类似的优势，其中Gig劳动不会被允许。为他们的一部分，鸟和石灰表示，他们说他们已经迁移过了近年来的中断，并成为政府合作伙伴的符合要求。最近几代鸟的踏板车，如鸟类二，耐刮擦的银，比早期型号更竞争，而石灰的耐磨性也是如此。

“我看到了踏板车狂热发生的事情，”白色解释道。 “是的，人行道杂乱有所有这些问题，但只是看数字。乘坐更多的女性骑行，更多的低收入人群骑行，而且更加种族多样化。“白色感觉到在彭博年度的自行车倡导中遇到的微生物运动周围相同的能量。
 大流行的锁模带来了滑板车狂热到突然停止。 2020年3月中旬之后，没有人想分享任何东西，而且没有人去任何地方，踏板车的数据收集能力是无用的。在美国和欧洲，金属天鹅进入冬眠，这意味着从城市街道上移除数千名摩托车。
 裁员遵循整个行业，石灰最终失去了独角兽的地位。尽管如此，当白人收到一封来自鸟类管理的电子邮件，让他在2020年3月26日召唤他到Zoom网络研讨会时，他没有透门。一个女人的声音读了一份声明，统称超过四百只鸟类员工，包括放大呼叫中的每个人。 （群众终端保留在YouTube上。）“它非常野蛮，”白人说。在九十秒钟结束后，立即在公司发布的笔记本电脑上屏幕，人们一直在家里工作，去了灰色，每个人都被锁定了电子邮件和松懈。
 他说，白人是“真的很低”，他说，他认为完全留下了运输领域。 2019年，他和他的妻子，Zoe Ryder，诗人，三个孩子搬到了阿尔斯特县六英亩的农场。他有很多项目。但是，随着锁上的锁上和踏板车分享到2020年夏天和秋天的城市，他开始游说加入超级节。
 “我想为好人工作，”他说。 “我只是在这个个人上投入了这么多。我觉得如果我们不赢得纽约，我将填补阿尔斯特县交通部的坑洼。“
 电动踏板车看起来不像交通的革命，而是对霍勒斯奉献，商业分析师和米科西州的领先的福音师 - 他创造了他们上诉的一部分。 “交通的下一次革命将来自底部，”Dediu表示。 Dediu出生于罗马尼亚，并作为一个孩子来到美国;他参加了塔夫茨和哈佛大学的商学院。他现在住在芬兰，在那里他是多峰的。在YouTube上，他在骑自行车时哲学了关于城市移动性的哲学。
 Dediu认为，就像沉重的台式电脑已被打火机，平板电脑和智能手机取代一样，所以汽车将“不捆绑”变为更轻，更清洁，更少的资源相关的EV，可以用于大部分旅行人们现在由汽车制作。 （在美国，所有汽车旅行中的六十百分之少于六英里。）锂离子电池，首先由索尼在高端摄像机中引入消费者，今天为您提供了一系列膨胀的移动设备 - 不仅仅是我们的手机和笔记本电脑还有车辆，如E-Bikes，E-Scooters，E-Monowheels，电子滑板和其他不断发展的微生物形式，即不再要求用户的能量移动它们。
